artists.name: Couple Two T'ree
artists.info:
How  would  you  describe  your  show?We're  both  pretty  deep  in  soul  and  funk  45s  so  that  tends  to  make  up  the  majority  of  our  shows,  but  we  also  collect  other  genres:  Latin,  Jamaican,  garage  rock,  psych,  city  pop,  etc.  We  both  have  a  similar  sensibility  towards  record  collecting  in  terms  of  what  we  listen  for,  the  focus  being  on  obscure  sounds. 
 If  you’re  in  a  collective,  how  did  you  meet  each  other? 
 I  (Joe)  have  been  playing  in  punk  and  hardcore  bands  since  I  was  14,  and  I'vebeen  collecting  records  for  just  as  long.  My  taste  grew  as  a  record  collector/enthusiast  out  a  desire  to  be  a  better,  well  informed  musician.  I  still  play  in  a  hardcore  band,  The  Lion's  Cage,  and  even  though  the  music  I  createis  pretty  different  than  what  I  buy,  collect  and  DJ  the  two  play  a  large  role  in  informing  each  other. 
 If  you  have  guests,  how  do  you  find  and  determine  guests  for  your  show?  There's  a  decent  amount  of  45  collectors  living  in  NY,  people  I've  been  good  friends  over  the  years  through  the  45  scene.  I  often  ask  those  friend's  to  fill  in  on  the  show,  people  I  know  who  have  a  similar  style  to  Couple  Two  T'ree.  Each  of  those  collectors  has  a  slightly  different  sound  in  what  they  look  for  in  45s,  if  you  listen  carefully  to  can  start  to  pick  out  a  DJ's  "sound."  Often  we  end  up  attributing  45s  to  that  DJ,  like  "Oh,  this  sounds  like  a  Joe  45"  because  it  fits  pretty  well  into  my  individual  sensibility.  This  leaves  room  for  guests  to  come  on  the  show  that  fit  the  format  but  also  bring  something  new  and  interesting  to  the  table. 
 How  does  the  Lot  feel  different  than  other  radio  stations? 
 The  Lot  is  different  in  so  many  ways  but  primarily  I'd  say  the  diversity  of  DJs  and  the  emphasis  on  passion.  We  were  one  of  the  earliest  regular  shows  on  the  station  and  I  remember  Francois  giving  us  some  praise  for  our  apparent  passion  for  what  we  do.  That  always  stuck  with  me  and  I've  realized  that's  magic  potion  for  what  has  made  The  Lot  so  amazing.  Every  single  DJ,  guest  or  regular,  that  blesses  that  shipping  container  is  abundantly  passionate  about  music.  That's  what  brought  Alston  and  I  together  as  friends,  our  mutual  passion  for  collecting  45s  and  DJing  and  that's  what  drives  us  to  do  this.show 
 In  what  ways  has  your  involvement  with  the  Lot  changed  how  you  think  about  music  today? 
 The  Lot  has  facilitated  a  super  solid  community  of  DJs  and  collectors  and  through  that  I've  met  some  incredible  people.  That  sense  of  community  has  made  me  even  more  stoked  to  be  doing  the  show  and  sharing  my  collection.  On  the  flip  The  Lot  has  also  opened  my  mind  to  some  new  stuff  that  I  was  never  into,  house  music  specifically.  Recently  started  going  down  that  hole  and  that's  something  I  would  have  never  saw  myself  getting  into  a  few  years  ago. 
 Are  you  connected  with  your  listeners?  If  so,  how? 
 Every  Sunday  there's  the  same  handful  of  listeners  active  in  the  chatroom,  shout  out  Biker  Bob,  Jorg,  Dud,  Toast,  NIC!  It's  pretty  incredible  to  have  real  time  interaction  with  listeners,  to  share  information  about  the  records  and  just  shoot  the  shit.  It's  actually  one  of  my  favorite  things  about  doing  the  show. 
 How  has  the  art  of  DJing  changed  with  the  rise  of  streaming  services? 
 For  me  personally  the  cameras  were  the  big  variable  in  terms  of  live  stream  DJing.  For  a  few  reasons.  I've  never  really  considered  myself  a  DJ,  I'm  not  very  good  from  a  technical  perspective,  but  since  the  inception  of  CTT  I've  made  a  point  to  get  better.  Knowing  that  people  are  listening  and  watching  itcan  be  kind  of  embarrassing  if  you  mess  up.  Second,  there's  a  little  bit  of  a  pressure  to  put  on  a  performance.  It's  not  always  the  most  interesting  to  just  watch  two  dudes  fuck  around  and  nerd  out  with  some  45s  on  a  live  stream,  but  if  those  two  dudes  are  clapping  and  dancing  and  genuinely  having  a  good  time  it's  a  different  story.  Alston  and  I  are  both  super  animate  when  we  DJ  out,  and  the  same  goes  for  when  were  on  air.  I  want  people  to  see  us  on  the  live  stream  and  go  "those  fucking  dudes  love  what  their  doing." 
 How  do  you  see  The  Lot  evolving? 
 It's  hard  to  picture  where  The  Lot  will  be  in  a  year,  or  two,  or  three.  When  it  first  started  it  was  pretty  bare  bones  and  reflecting  on  how  far  it  has  come  in  the  past  two  years  its  unbelievable.  We  recently  had  our  two  year  anniversary  party  at  Brooklyn  Bazaar.  That  night  was  the  most  transformative  night  I've  ever  had  as  a  DJ,  a  collector,  a  musician,  and  a  music  lover.  There  were  so  many  people  are  that  party  that  you  could  barely  walk  from  room  to  room  the  whole  night.  It's  a  difficult  feeling  to  describeIt's  hard  to  picture  where  The  Lot  will  be  in  a  year,  or  two,  or  three.  When  it  first  started  it  was  pretty  bare  bones  and  reflecting  on  how  far  it  has  come  in  the  past  two  years  its  unbelievable.  We  recently  had  our  two  year  anniversary  party  at  Brooklyn  Bazaar.  That  night  was  the  most  transformative  night  I've  ever  had  as  a  DJ,  a  collector,  a  musician,  and  a  music  lover.  There  were  so  many  people  are  that  party  that  you  could  barely  walk  from  room  to  room  the  whole  night.  It's  a  difficult  feeling  to  describe but that  set  we  DJed  at  the  party  was  the  most  important  thing  I've  ever  done  as  a  DJ,  I  felt  legitimately  part  of  something  beautiful  and  important  in  a  way  I  have  never  before.  It  was  a  truly  affirming  and  positive  experience.  So  after  that  it's  hard  to  picture  where  the  station  will  go  in  the  next  few  years,  but  Francois  is  a  genius  and  all  the  people  involved  in  making  The  Lot  happen  are  so  driven  and  passionate  to  spread  the  message.  Wherever  it's  going  from  here  is  going  to  be  tremendous.

artists.name: Camille Vourzay
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll. 
 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 
 I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work! 
 Name: Camille VourzayShow Name: Camille BWRHometown: Clermont­Ferrand, France; Paris, France. Years of DJing: 5 yearsHow would you describe your show? It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll.What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work!If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 
 Most of the time they are friends and/or coworkers or DJs that I met at their gig and I invited them. 
 Name: Camille VourzayShow Name: Camille BWRHometown: Clermont­Ferrand, France; Paris, France. Years of DJing: 5 yearsHow would you describe your show? It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll.What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work!If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show?Most of the time they are friends and/or coworkers or DJs that I met at their gig and I invited them.How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 
 It’s different because it mixes a really large spectrum of people but you still feel it’s the same big family in a way. 
 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 
 It didn’t change my involvement, but it pushes me to have new records almost each time and be better (at least trying to!) 
 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 
 A bit yes, by the different social networks and sometimes I meet them during a set. 
 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 
 It’s the contradiction spirit. The more the streaming services rise, the more I’m gonna tryto find some tunes that they are not on those streaming services!

artists.name: Charlotte von Kotze
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 Brooklyn English is obviously a pun full of irony celebrating accents. It came up as I was discussing about my accent and cultural identities with friends. More importantly, the show is all about an eclectic mix of tracks from traditional and contemporary music/musicians (known, emerging or unknown). The goal is to break categorizations and borders with different styles from the 6 continents; i have also really felt that need after the last presidential elections. I usually start more downtempo/instrumental/ambient, I then move to more traditional and percussive music: recently from Japan and Africa; I'll usually continue the BPM increase through music from Central , Brazil, South America and at 11, I'll either have a guest or go more international house with a touch of afrobeat, afrohouse here and there and towards the end, we'll finish more with jazz or some mid-downtempo new jams from new artists who recently sent me their music (recently from Australia and Iceland). The exciting thing is that i have so many more bands and countries to  research and feature! 

 Occasionally, I will have my friend Erica Roden joining the show (she co-owns the Bed-Stuy record store The Mixtape Shop) but I also have guests who carry an eclectic and international story such as Malcolm Mooney (CAN), Composer Chassol, Jason Lindner ( Now vs Now, collaborations with Donny McCaslin, David Bowie, Mulatu Astatke), Kurt Uenala (for his own project and his current /  past collabs with Depeche Mode) Sam Valenti (Ghostly International founder), Hat (Remix-Culture), Congolese Artist Pierre Kwenders (Moonshine Montreal, Trumpetist Takuya Nakamura, and Bastard Jazz (Lord Echo, Illa J). On the side, I have also taken my show to the next level when I curated a concert featuring Vincent Moon, Priscilla Telmon, Laraaji and a premiere about an Alice Coltrane documentary with The Lot at the San Damiano Mission. We are trying to do more of these in 2018. What’s your relationship with music? 

 (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? Really since I was born: I am initially classically trained (clarinet), I then evolved to a more jazz background with my family who also plays music and eventually started working within the music industry for the last 10 years: as a project manager in the export of international music, then as an indie promoter and for Giant Step, and for the last 4 years, I have been doing Music Supervision. On the side, I curate shows here and there and slowly start learning new instruments as the creation bug is contagious;. 

 How does the daily life inform my show? 

The show is featuring music I discover on the day-to-day, a lot by word of mouth from friends and artists I contacted around the world, but also through exhibitions, books, actively or randomly digging (I recently found a  rare  old 45 vinyl of an anonymous Egyptian artist while I was on fruit market in Paris) and of course travels: Every time I travel (recently in Cuba, France, Germany, Portugal and in a few months, Ghana), I'm trying to meet with the locals to better understand the traditional music scene and culture, that's also the best way to have an authentic travel experience.  

If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

I find and determine the guests the same way you dj: by trusting my gut! It's a lot word of mouth, by following the shows happening in New York so I can offer a platform to them if they are available. I also build topics here and there depending on the season. Bottom line, these guests are echoing eclecticism: they are/were part of big projects and/or have their own projects, they educate us and have a connexion with culture, identity, the world, and history and i try to give them a voice as there is so much we can musically (and also on the human level) learn from them. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 You do have internet radios but The Lot is the only radio I know who was built thanks to a collaboration with friars / the San Damiano Mission. Also The Lot is one of the rare radios which has a physical community. Paris has Le Mellotron set up at a bar but The Lot built its own cafe, bar, garden. community container and an identity; so no wonder that when they do an event like the 4th of July party, more than 1,000 people are joining. It's only been 2 years but they have achieved so much despite a small team and it's just the beginning. Francois trusted his gut, worked crazy hard with his team and he can be so proud of  him and them. People meet there to catch up with friends but also to exchange about their projects.The Lot does invite big DJs and renown artists but they also trust the voice and tastes of young curators as long as they have ideas and dedications; they really give a voice to many and diversity which result in more education and discoveries for an audience who won't let their choices being dictated by mainstream radios. I want to take this opportunity to deeply thank San Damiano, the whole team especially Francois and Pauline who work days and night and a lot behind the curtain; they are crucial to the project and New York owe them; also the baristas who are always around with their good mood and their photo art directions. THANK YOU! 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

It has deepened my relationship with music: more active listenings to match melodies, beats, and tones; it pushes me to also understand better the music technicalities of composers, their creative process, but also of the mixer, of switching between vinyl and CDJs, of doing smooth transitions. I also mainly consider myself lucky to have this show with Francois, Pauline, and Chris' help . In return, I can't take everything for granted and have to make sure the audience is excited: it pushes me to be curious, to dig for more music when i travel, it pushes me to get specific guests and unique topics (soon, I'll do a couple of shows about music and cinema, a marriage which fascinates me), and to deeply prepare my shows. It should not just be music I like but a curation for the audience. Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 A lot through the chat during the show (I do have some loyal listeners from Brazil who have been listening since Day 1) but also through socials, I'll get some requests after the show regarding specific tracks, archives or even questions regarding guests. It's always a good feeling when someone comes to you at a concert and tells you that they recognize you from the show and love it. It warms you up to know people care about less obvious styles of music. I thank anybody who tuned/tune/or will tune in and of course the guests. On a funny note, a lot of friends have recently thanked me for brightening up their Sundays when they had to work or clean their houses! 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 It has opened doors to more people to join the art of DJing, not just the ones who have the connexions or the big reputation. People are more connected, there are more ways to feature unknown artist and even remix them. There is more authenticity and curation but also desire to go beyond the borders of the mainstream FM/AM system which is dictated a lot by music priorities from the industries. With streaming, there is more freedom of speech and diversity. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 With bigger community because the radio will extend their activity and collaboration with more events, more shows, more festivals and maybe with more radio. On the curation level, i think they will keep having a mix of emerging hosts and artists as well as renown DJs and musicians with shows being about music and / or with interviews.
artists.years: I have actively been collecting music for the last 8 years. I have been curating music for videos (Music Supervision) for the last 4­5 years and I have been DJing / playing music publicly at The Lot and here and there at some locations in the neighborhood for the last year (Achilles’ Heel, Donna Brooklyn, Project Parlor...etc.) However, I am not a regular professional DJ playing at clubs on the day­to­day.

artists.name: Christine Renee
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A release, a comedy, a tragedy, a visual and visceral connection to groovie beats that span the space/time continuum. 

 What’s your relationship with music? 

 DJ Sound Artist. 

 In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 The daily has to be present. Music coming from that source of “nowness” is relevant and more powerful. 

 If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

 I love to invite people who don’t play on The Lot, who are not from the NY area and who may not be currently playing out. Anytime I can share the talents of friends with a broader audience, I get excited to do so. This is usually what informs my choice in DJ guests. I’ll also invite visual artists, performers and dancers on to the show. Sometimes the Disco Chicken drops in. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 The community. I think it’s about that first in some ways, so you have music coming from half of a Greenpoint coffee shop. The holy Coffee Shop has always been central to artistic populations. Of course, this links fellow DJs and musicians to each other, and also to the community, ie., the passerby that wants a cup of brew. Then you add the ability to have an interactive, online and visual connection internationally, and your community increases infinitely. There are a lot of layers of goodness. 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 Having a weekly radio show forced me to play music that I hadn’t had time to internalize to the extent to which I might have in the past when filling my bag for a club. It was a real gift, because then I took less ownership of the songs I played; they became “for others,” more fluid. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 I’d say so. I love seeing familiar names in the chat room and I have gotten to meet some of the people from there. Also, I feel connected energetically. Even though there isn’t a visual audience you still feel how the listeners are receiving it. 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 Maybe that there are more DJs and more music available now than I’ve ever seen before, and this has helped people find their micro niches. A DJ doesn’t have to play club music these days. So maybe it’s allowed people to become more personal in their selections. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 I see it growing like an oak tree--rooting down more with it’s community and growing up and out to people all over the globe.

artists.name: Cyrus Sarrafha
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 An attempt to provide an informative and pleasant listening experience, showcasing the boundaries of progressive rock and improvised jazz. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 An enthusiast and a completist disc jockey. An architect by day. 

 If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other? 

 Co-founder and lyricist of Stone Float, a collaborative duo project with my college friend, Hosain Paknezhad. www.soundcloud.com/stone-float 

 If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

 Usually there is so much I want to share with listeners that I run out of time for having guests. though I think it'll be great to have guests who are interested and/or have knowledgeable in prog rock. However, I have had my good friend Luis Padron as my guest on The Lot twice. www.soundcloud.com/luis-pardon 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 Beside the fact that The Lot is run by awesome friends, the main reason I love Lot is that they insist on entertaining the idea of giving all kinds of music a chance--regardless of any genre. It has never been an easy task for me to arrange a similar type of program on any other radio show, except LPH Radio (thank you Nic Mercer!) who invited me on his show couple of years ago. www.soundcloud.com/lphnyc/lph-radioep-xxxxiiistone-floats-cyrus-sarrafha 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 I'm usually in disagreement when someone says "there is not enough good music to listen to these days" or "there is no new good music." In fact, there is so much good music out there--both old and new--that I believe, life is simply too short to waste it by listening to bad music. Now, what is considered "bad music", is purely based on one's taste and their personal experiences, mostly driven by memories and nostalgia. Even our attempts to explore new genres has its roots in what we have learned before. It all boils down to a clear understanding of "how is music actually important to you." It is the degree of importance, of the quality of your listening experience that defines your taste. Do you listen to music when travelling to work on daily basis? And does it truly move you to do something great? Does it bring you to a better understanding of yourself? Are you actively listening, or merely hearing it? The answers to these questions move me to prepare what I consider to deserve more attention, and to share with the listeners. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 It's the music that connects us. 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 I believe the art of DJing involves having many technical abilities as well as an understanding of how to keep your listener excited. I do lack the first half of this formula. So, personally, I don't think it's fair to call myself a DJ. It will be unfair to those who have been doing this for a long time and have mastered--or even, created--all necessary technical requirements. Do I call myself a DJ, as in a disc jockey? Yes, of course. Choosing to buy music over food and being eager to share that experience with others, have happened on numerous occasions! But can I make a roomful of thousands of people dance? No, of course not. But if you just relax in your favorite chair and have your drink and cigar in hand, I can take you somewhere you may have never been before. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 Keep at it! You guys are doing great!
artists.years: Since April 19th, 2015 at Bossa Nova Civic Club

artists.name: Dave Harrington
artists.info:
How would you describe your show?  

 Totally free form.  I like to think of it as simply playing 2 hours of what I feel like listening to, whatever I’m listening at the time or want to spend some more time with.  But, that being said, I’ve taken to screening old movies on the video stream, so sometimes the film influences what I play, or how I mix and move amongst different music.  I also love the occasional theme show too. Recently I did a show that was all Impulse jazz records from the 50s and 60s. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 I’m a multi-instrumentalist, producer, and composer. I play in a band called Darkside, I lead my own groups and collaborate with lottos NYC musicians these days mostly in improvised music settings. So even though I’m surrounded by music all the time, there is something special about my shows on The Lot. I think of it as a time where ALL I’m going to do is listening to music, no interruptions, no business, just pure listening. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 The Lot is amazing because it has managed to seamlessly integrate itself into the music community in New York while simultaneously growing that community and helping musicians and DJs and show promoters meet and hang.  It’s one of the few places in the city where I know that I will always run into someone I know, and more often than not, meet someone new. 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 I really believe that music isn’t just a passion or a job, at its best, it is a true community.  This is something I have tried to foster and build in my own way in NYC and The Lot has helped me in that ongoing, never-ending project and also shown me that that ethos is really truly possible. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 There are certain regulars who usually pop up in the chat room during my shows (you know who you are!) and they always have really positive stuff to say.  I love how friendly and funny and wild the chatroom can be.  Whereas so much of the internet is so different, I feel like it’s just really special that the most controversial conversations during my show usually revolve around people opinions about Jerry Garcia when I start playing Grateful Dead tunes. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 Who knows?! But all I can say is that I want to continue to be a part of it as it grows, whatever that looks like.  It’s just too fun!
artists.show_name:
Dave Harrington, though I keep thinking I want to change it to 
“CINEMA!
”
artists.years: 13 years since I briefly had a show on WKCR during the one semester Ispent at Columbia University.

artists.name: DJ Lindsey
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A mix of soulful jams from the past, present and future.

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) 

      I have worked as a DJ full-time for 10 years. Any job before and during has just been a means to allow me to continue to DJ and afford life in NY. 

 In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 I am listening to music and thinking about music most of the day, either for pleasure or for work. When I'm not doing that I'm singing in the shower or to my kids. It's a constant in my house/life. 

      How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

      It's way more of a community than the other station I did a show on. Everyone's friendly and you feel supported. I used to harass Lloyd and Francois about my stats. "How many people are listening every month?" They used to laugh at me and not answer. I finally relaxed a little, and I really think my show is more interesting because I don't have the pressure that I felt before on my old show on another station. It's just way more natural, way more quality control goes into each show on my part because I can do exactly what I want. I don't feel like I have to please anyone and it can just be for people who enjoy my perspective on music. 

      In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

      I am able to listen to and share way more quality stuff on my show. When I do play out at a bar now, I feel way more pressure than ever to please the crowd. Bars don't really have the ability to nurture a night and let it gradually build like they used to so I really have to bend to the whims of the crowd. I'm not a marquee headlining DJ so people don't necessarily come out waiting to hear what I'm gonna play. A lot of times I get booked because I am good (toot) and local and they know I will show up and be professional. If I was only doing stuff like that, I wouldn't be able to play slower stuff, older stuff, weirder stuff. So doing my show on The Lot is still a lot of fun for me, I get to just scour the internet for new artists/producers, old gems and support the stuff my friends are doing. 

      Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

      I definitely utilize the chat during my show. It's fun to see what folks think of the music in real time and whatever random gifs and conversations pop up. I promote my show on social media and when I have time I send out a tracklist of songs from my previous shows so people know what’s happening and when. 

      How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

      I don't know that streaming services has changed DJing. I saw a big jump around 2010-2011 when everyone started using controllers and digital DJing became the industry standard and more affordable. I'm curious to see what happens when iTunes ends though. I know Serato made an attempt at integrating Pulselocker into their software but not sure how that went. At this point a lot of DJs are just using USB sticks and organizing music at home before a gig. It feels like a lot of folks are in the process of figuring out easier ways to transport and organize music. Having it all there for you could be good if you're able to organize things in your mind on the fly. For some folks like me, having some parameters is better. 

      How do you see The Lot evolving? 

      I just see it getting bigger---big guests dropping in to guest, bigger DJs doing shows, and a bigger community component which is what I see being a priority for the station.
